hypotheses.title: The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month accrual read as an annual snapshot — the pre-launch clock makes even that generous
hypotheses.narrative: obtainableFactor's 1% was 'corrected' in refinement cycle 1 and now looks conservative and triangulated. But the benchmark corpus it leans on defines SOM as a 1–3 year (Prospeo, IdeaPlan) or 3–5 year (Wise) cumulative capture, not a Year-1 run-rate, and the one worked example in the corpus lands at 0.1% of SAM — 5x below the band's own floor. Layer the venture's actual clock on top: it is pre-launch, so months 1–6 are certification, listing and pipeline-building, not selling. A 'conservative ramp' that books its first PO in month 7–9 doesn't earn 1% of SAM; it earns a fraction of the back half of the year. The band's low edge (0.5%) is not the pessimistic case — it is the optimistic reading of the sources' own semantics.

mechanism.text: The Prospeo/IdeaPlan/Wise benchmark family defines the 1–5% obtainable share as accruing over 1–3 or 3–5 years, not per-year; the ledger pins 1% strictly to Year 1

The only concrete worked example in the corpus yields SOM/SAM ≈ 0.1% ($3.2M/$3.2B) for years 1–2, and the Tractian comparable started at $200K — not the ~$1.2M first-year figure the ledger's crossCheck asserts
The venture is pre-launch with a conservative-ramp profile: the selling window inside Year 1 is 6–9 months at best, so any annualized benchmark must be pro-rated before it is applied
refs: profile:What obtainable horizon should Year-1 (YAM) assume?

mechanism.text: obtainableFactor at 0.01 overstates the realistic Year-1 capture by 2–3x; the defensible point sits at or below the band floor

hypotheses.whyMissable: The node was already corrected once (3%→1% in refinement cycle 1) and carries maturity 'triangulated' — it reads as the fixed, conservative number. The residual error is not the value but the time-semantics of the sources it cites, which no band edge encodes.
hypotheses.falsifier: A comparable early-stage DC-hardware entrant (intelligent PDU or adjacent rack power gear) documented capturing ≥1% of its serviceable market within its first 12 calendar months from launch.

likelihood.rationale: Three of four benchmark sources in the digest explicitly frame the percentage as multi-year, and the single worked example sits 5x below the band floor; the offsetting uncertainty is that the venture may have pre-launch pipeline the model doesn't credit.

perturbation.note: Pro-rated Year-1 capture consistent with cumulative-window benchmarks and the 0.1% worked example

indicators.signal: Months from launch to first booked CE purchase order
indicators.where: Own CRM plus TED (EU public tender portal) responses to rack-power lots the venture bids

indicators.signal: First-year revenue disclosures of comparable rack-power/DC-hardware entrants
indicators.where: Crunchbase/annual filings of recent PDU or rack-infrastructure startups entering EU
indicators.threshold: Comparables clustering below 0.5% of their stated SAM in year 1

mitigations.action: Build a bottom-up pipeline model (named accounts × win rate × cycle time × months of selling window) to replace the top-down 1% before fundraising commitments are set against YAM
mitigations.note: Directly replaces the contested benchmark with venture-specific arithmetic

mitigations.action: Re-baseline the internal plan on a 24-month obtainable window with quarterly gates, so Year-1 misses don't cascade into a credibility problem with the board

hypotheses.title: The firmware/security audit gate precedes every intelligent-PDU listing — and it isn't in the funnel
hypotheses.narrative: An intelligent PDU is a networked device sitting on the management LAN of critical infrastructure. Before any large CE operator issues a PO, the vendor must clear a security prequalification that a dumb-metal vendor never faces: vulnerability disclosure process, signed-firmware update infrastructure, SBOM, penetration evidence, increasingly IEC 62443-style artifacts as the EU Cyber Resilience Act obligations phase in. This gate is serial with — not parallel to — CE/TÜV product certification, and it is audited against infrastructure the pre-launch venture has to build during the very year YAM assumes it is harvesting. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut names only the classic cert stack; the security gate is a second, slower door in front of the same customers, and it opens per-operator, not per-product.

mechanism.text: Large-operator prequalification for networked power devices requires an auditable firmware update/PSIRT/SBOM capability before AVL listing; the documented EU entry stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, FCC) is already broader than the ledger's derivation names, and cyber requirements sit on top of it

A pre-launch entrant builds this infrastructure and passes per-operator audits inside the same 12 months YAM covers; each audit cycle runs one to two quarters, and the primary-buyer choice (large operators) selects exactly the buyers with the heaviest security gates
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

mechanism.text: The gate serially delays the first PO (compressing the effective selling window inside obtainableFactor) and forecloses security-gated accounts entirely for Year 1 (a reach shortfall inside serviceableFactor) — neither effect appears in the static 0.55 or the 1%

hypotheses.whyMissable: The founder's risk list has a 'certifications' line item, and it looks handled — CE/TÜV are known, scheduled, budgeted. The security audit is invisible from the outside because it is buyer-side process, not published standard: it appears for the first time as a 40-page vendor questionnaire attached to the first serious RFQ, after the model's clock has already started.
hypotheses.falsifier: Two or more large CE operators (or their integrators) issuing rack-PDU RFQs in the venture's first two quarters with no firmware-security/SBOM prequalification requirement, or confirming that product CE marking alone suffices for AVL listing.

likelihood.rationale: Security prequalification for networked infrastructure devices is standard practice at hyperscale/large-colo buyers and CRA obligations are phasing in, but mid-market and distributor channels gate far less strictly; probability reflects the venture's explicit large-operator-first choice.

perturbation.note: Security-gated large accounts unreachable within Year 1 despite product certification
First-PO delay of ~2 quarters halves the effective Year-1 selling window

indicators.signal: Vendor-security questionnaire content (SBOM, signed firmware, PSIRT, 62443 mapping) in rack-power prequalification packets
indicators.where: Procurement/supplier portals of Equinix, Digital Realty, NorthC and other CE colo operators; first three RFQs the venture receives
indicators.threshold: ≥2 of first 3 RFQs requiring SBOM or firmware-security attestation before quotation

indicators.signal: CRA horizontal-standard timeline for network-connected industrial products
indicators.where: CEN/CENELEC JTC13 docket and Commission CRA implementing-act publications
indicators.threshold: Harmonized standard citing connected power-distribution devices with obligations landing inside the venture's first 24 months

mitigations.action: Run a mock security audit against two target operators' published supplier requirements pre-launch to size the true listing lead time per account
mitigations.note: Converts an unknown serial gate into a scheduled one; de-risks the Year-1 timing assumption

mitigations.action: Ship with a third-party-audited firmware signing and PSIRT process at launch (certifiable artifact, not a roadmap slide), and sequence first sales through channels with lighter security gates (distributors, mid-market) while operator audits run

hypotheses.title: Global framework agreements make the 0.40 large-operator cell mostly non-contestable in the venture's window
hypotheses.narrative: The model's biggest customer cell — large operators at 0.40 of PDU spend — encodes the assumption 'large operators buy direct, therefore a direct-selling entrant can compete for them.' But the way large operators buy direct is through multi-year global framework agreements and integrated infrastructure packages with incumbents whose customer lists already span every hyperscaler and major colo. A framework doesn't shrink the cell's spend; it forecloses it: the PDU line is decided once, globally, years at a time, and never comes to regional tender. For a pre-launch regional entrant whose profile explicitly targets large operators first, the nominal 0.40 collapses to the sliver of that spend outside framework coverage — while the model's YAM math quietly credits the whole cell as contestable.

mechanism.text: Incumbent rack-power vendors hold entrenched direct relationships with the exact buyers in this cell — Vertiv's disclosed major-customer list alone covers Equinix and the global telcos/hyperscalers — and data-center demand is concentrating supplier order books around these accounts

The 0.40 value itself is an unsourced internal estimate: no retrieved source substantiates the channel split, and the one hard number cited (30% of one supplier's orders) measures order composition, not buyer-type contestability — so the cell carries no evidence about how much of it ever reaches open competition

If framework-covered spend is removed, the large-operator cell available to a new regional entrant in its first 12–24 months is roughly half its nominal size, and the venture's chosen go-to-market points at the most-foreclosed cell first
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

hypotheses.whyMissable: 'Large operators buy direct' sounds like an access advantage — no distributor margin, a named-account sales motion the founder knows how to run. The foreclosure hides inside the same fact: direct purchasing at scale means framework purchasing, and framework spend never appears as a losable deal, so the pipeline simply looks mysteriously thin rather than visibly blocked.
hypotheses.falsifier: Three CE large-operator procurement interviews (or won/lost tender records) showing rack-PDU purchases regularly competed line-by-line at regional level outside global frameworks — e.g., annual PDU bake-offs open to non-incumbent vendors.

likelihood.rationale: Framework and integrated-package procurement by large operators is documented behavior in the digest corpus and incumbent customer lists overlap the target accounts almost completely, but the actual framework-coverage share of PDU spend is unquantified — the cell value itself is admitted guesswork in both directions.

perturbation.note: Roughly half of nominal large-operator PDU spend sits under multi-year frameworks/integrated packages and is non-contestable in the venture's window

indicators.signal: New multi-year power-infrastructure framework or integrated-rack supply announcements naming CE operators
indicators.where: Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton quarterly earnings calls and press rooms; operator capex-day presentations
indicators.threshold: ≥1 new CE-relevant operator signing a global power framework covering rack distribution

indicators.signal: Standalone rack-PDU lots appearing (or not) in operator tenders, and their bidder counts
indicators.where: TED (EU tender portal) and operator supplier portals for DE/NL colo fit-outs
indicators.threshold: Standalone PDU lots absent, sole-sourced, or drawing ≤2 bidders across two consecutive quarters

mitigations.action: Commission the three CE operator/distributor procurement interviews already named in the ledger's promotionPath, specifically instrumented to measure framework coverage of PDU spend
mitigations.note: Replaces the unsourced 0.40 with a contestability-adjusted split

mitigations.action: Re-sequence go-to-market to lead with colocation mid-market and OEM/integrator channels where line-item bake-offs still occur, holding large operators as a Year-2+ motion entered via a framework-holder partnership

hypotheses.title: The growth the CAGR promises was electrically specified before launch day — Year 1 can only sell into the installed base
hypotheses.narrative: Nine percent CAGR reads as a tailwind: new halls, new racks, new PDU line items every quarter of Year 1. But new-build DC electrical procurement is locked at design freeze, and with long-lead power equipment (transformers, switchgear) now booked years out, operators are locking entire electrical BOMs — PDUs riding along — far earlier than the 12–24-month window the ledger's own crossCheck uses to justify its ceiling. Every megawatt that energizes during the venture's first 12 months had its rack-power vendor chosen while the venture was still pre-launch. That leaves Year-1 demand confined to retrofit, refresh and expansion churn in the installed base — a materially smaller and slower pool than 'the market' — while the model applies its 1% to the whole SAM as if new-build spend were reachable on the venture's clock.

mechanism.text: New-build electrical BOMs for capacity energizing in the venture's Year 1 were specified at design freeze, before the venture existed as a qualified vendor; the ledger's own crossCheck concedes gear is 'locked 12–24mo before fit-out' and current long-lead conditions push lock-in earlier still

The market's growth is concentrated in exactly this pre-committed new-build pipeline (the substance of the 9% CAGR), so the contestable Year-1 pool is the installed base's refresh cycle — and what standalone PDU procurement evidence exists shows small, sometimes sole-sourced lots with 1–2 bidders, not open bake-offs

Generic obtainable-share benchmarks (1% Year 1) assume the full SAM is time-reachable; no DC-hardware-specific benchmark exists that discounts for procurement lock-in, so the model has no mechanism to encode it
obtainableFactor applied to the full SAM double-counts pre-committed new-build spend as reachable; the truly contestable Year-1 share sits at or below the band floor

hypotheses.whyMissable: The founder's plan treats the CAGR as an accelerant and 1% as conservative precisely because the market is growing — 'we only need a tiny slice of a rising tide.' The trap is that the rising portion of the tide is the least reachable portion on a 12-month clock; the model has no node that separates committed-pipeline demand from contestable demand, so nothing in the ledger flags it.
hypotheses.falsifier: Documented cases of CE new-build data centers running competitive rack-PDU selection within 6 months of fit-out (i.e., inside the venture's selling window), or an operator confirming PDUs are procured late as a commodity line decoupled from the electrical design freeze.

likelihood.rationale: Early electrical lock-in for new builds is well-documented industry behavior and the ledger's own crossCheck relies on a 12–24-month version of it; the offsetting uncertainty is how much PDU spend is genuinely late-cycle commodity purchasing versus BOM-committed — unquantified in every retrieved source.

perturbation.note: Year-1 capture limited to installed-base retrofit/refresh; new-build pipeline pre-committed before launch

indicators.signal: Stage at which rack-PDU vendor is fixed in CE fit-out tenders (bundled at design freeze vs late separate lot)
indicators.where: TED tender documents for DE/NL data-center fit-outs; fit-out contractors' bid packages
indicators.threshold: PDU line bundled into general-electrical packages in ≥3 of 4 sampled tenders

indicators.signal: Pre-let percentages and long-lead procurement commentary for CE capacity additions
indicators.where: Operator and hyperscaler capex calls (Equinix, Digital Realty quarterly); Vertiv/Schneider backlog commentary
indicators.threshold: Pre-let >80% with electrical packages cited as booked >18 months out

mitigations.action: Map the 2026–27 CE new-build pipeline by procurement stage (design freeze reached? electrical package awarded?) to quantify the actually-contestable Year-1 pool
mitigations.note: Separates committed from contestable SAM — the distinction the funnel lacks

mitigations.action: Target the installed-base refresh cycle explicitly: build the launch offer around drop-in replacement and retrofit economics (metering upgrades, DCIM integration) rather than new-build spec wins

hypotheses.title: MID/Eichrecht-grade metering gates the billing use case in Germany — half the geography, the core intelligent-PDU value prop
hypotheses.narrative: The intelligent PDU's premium over dumb power strips rests substantially on outlet- and rack-level metering. But in Germany — 50% of the modeled geography — any meter whose readings are used to bill sub-tenants per measured kWh falls under legal-metrology obligations (MessEG/MID): the metering chain must be conformity-assessed, not merely accurate. A pre-launch entrant whose PDU metering is 'billing-grade' in spec but not MID-certified in law cannot sell the billing use case to German colos at all, and MID module certification is its own multi-quarter, notified-body process that is nowhere on the launch-critical path. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 bundles 'EU cert stack' into one qualitative haircut and never distinguishes this instrument — yet it forecloses the highest-value slice of demand in the largest country cell.

mechanism.text: German legal metrology (MessEG, implementing MID 2014/32/EU) requires conformity-assessed meters wherever measured kWh is billed to third parties — a distinct instrument absent from the ledger's named cert set, which the digest already shows is narrower than the real EU entry stack

Colocation operators re-billing power to tenants are precisely the buyers who pay the intelligent-PDU premium for metering; Germany is 0.50 of the geography split and colocation plus hyperscale wholesale re-billing spans the majority of the segment mix

MID module B+D certification via a notified body is a multi-quarter engineering-plus-audit effort; for a pre-launch venture it cannot complete inside the same Year 1 the funnel is harvesting, so the German billing-metering demand is foreclosed for the period, not merely delayed
serviceableFactor 0.55 silently includes billing-metering demand it cannot legally reach in DE during Year 1; the reachable share sits at the band floor or below until MID certification lands

hypotheses.whyMissable: The product meets or beats billing-class accuracy on its datasheet, so 'metering compliance' looks like a solved engineering problem. Legal metrology is a conformity regime, not an accuracy bar — a distinction invisible until a German colo's procurement lawyer asks for the MID declaration, by which point the tender is lost. And because much colo billing today is capacity-tier rather than per-kWh, early conversations can pass without the gate ever surfacing.
hypotheses.falsifier: Confirmation from two German colocation operators (or the responsible eichamt/PTB guidance) that PDU-level metering used for tenant re-billing is exempt from MessEG conformity requirements in their billing model, or that estimate/capacity-tier billing makes MID-certified PDU metering commercially irrelevant to their purchase decision.

likelihood.rationale: The legal obligation for billed per-kWh metering is clear, but its bite depends on how much CE colo billing is actually measured-kWh at PDU level versus capacity-tier or upstream branch-circuit metering — heterogeneity the corpus documents but does not quantify.

perturbation.note: German billing-metering demand foreclosed until MID conformity; reachable share drops to band floor

indicators.signal: Billing basis in German colo contract templates and tenders (measured per-kWh at rack vs fixed-kW capacity tiers)
indicators.where: German colo operators' published service schedules; DE colo lots on TED and operator supplier portals
indicators.threshold: Per-kWh sub-metered billing specified in ≥30% of sampled DE colo offerings

indicators.signal: MID/Eichrecht enforcement posture and guidance on data-center sub-metering
indicators.where: PTB publications and Länder eichamt enforcement notices; VfEB (Eichrecht compliance association) docket
indicators.threshold: Any published guidance or enforcement action explicitly covering DC tenant re-billing meters

mitigations.action: Obtain a legal-metrology opinion plus two German colo billing-practice interviews to determine what fraction of DE PDU demand actually requires MID-conformant metering
mitigations.note: Converts the bundled 0.55 haircut into an instrument-by-instrument reach model for the largest country cell

mitigations.action: Start MID module certification for the metering subsystem pre-launch in parallel with CE/TÜV, and ship a non-billing SKU positioning (monitoring/capacity-planning) for DE accounts until it lands
mitigations.note: Decouples first revenue from the slowest conformity gate
